quiz_questions.question_text: Which international agreement, adopted in 2015, aims to limit global warming and has influenced energy policies worldwide?

options.text: The Geneva Convention
The Kyoto Protocol
The Montreal Protocol
The Paris Agreement

quiz_questions.explanation: The Paris Agreement, adopted in 2015 by 196 parties, set the goal of limiting global temperature rise to well below 2 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, driving nations to adopt cleaner energy policies.

quiz_questions.question_text: Germany's Energiewende policy primarily focuses on which objective?

options.text: Importing all energy from neighboring countries
Expanding nuclear power capacity
Increasing coal production for export
Transitioning to renewable energy while phasing out nuclear and fossil fuels

quiz_questions.explanation: Germany's Energiewende (energy transition) is a long-term policy to shift the country's energy system from fossil fuels and nuclear to renewable sources while improving energy efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a carbon tax?

options.text: A tax on renewable energy installations
A fee imposed on the burning of carbon-based fuels proportional to their carbon content
A subsidy given to companies that use fossil fuels
A tariff on imported carbon fiber products

quiz_questions.explanation: A carbon tax charges producers or consumers a fee based on the carbon dioxide emissions generated by burning fossil fuels, creating a financial incentive to reduce emissions and shift toward cleaner energy sources.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which country was the first to implement a nationwide carbon tax in 1990?

options.text: Finland
Germany
Japan
United States

quiz_questions.explanation: Finland was the first country to introduce a carbon tax in 1990, establishing a pioneering policy that has since been adopted or considered by many other nations around the world.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of a Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS)?

options.text: To set a maximum price for renewable energy
To ban all fossil fuel use immediately
To subsidize oil and gas exploration
To require utilities to obtain a specified percentage of their electricity from renewable sources

quiz_questions.explanation: A Renewable Portfolio Standard mandates that electric utilities generate a certain percentage of their power from renewable energy sources by a target date, driving investment in wind, solar, and other clean technologies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happened at the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear power plant in 2011 that changed energy policy in several countries?

options.text: The plant set a world record for electricity production
A terrorist attack destroyed the reactor containment
The plant was converted to a solar power facility
An earthquake and tsunami caused a nuclear meltdown and radiation release

quiz_questions.explanation: The 2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami caused core meltdowns at the Fukushima Daiichi plant in Japan, leading to the worst nuclear disaster since Chernobyl and prompting several countries, notably Germany, to accelerate their nuclear phase-out plans.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a cap-and-trade system?

options.text: A policy that caps the price of fossil fuels
A system that places a limit on total emissions and allows companies to buy and sell emission permits
A trade agreement between countries for exchanging energy resources
A limit on the number of power plants a company can operate

quiz_questions.explanation: Cap-and-trade sets an overall limit (cap) on emissions, distributes or auctions emission allowances, and lets companies trade these permits. Companies that reduce emissions cheaply can sell excess permits to those facing higher abatement costs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which U.S. state has been a leader in implementing aggressive renewable energy and climate policies, including a 100% clean electricity target?

options.text: West Virginia
Texas
Alaska
California

quiz_questions.explanation: California has been at the forefront of U.S. energy and climate policy, establishing ambitious renewable energy mandates, cap-and-trade programs, and a goal of 100% clean electricity by 2045.

quiz_questions.question_text: What role do fossil fuel subsidies play in global energy policy?

options.text: They artificially lower the cost of fossil fuels, discouraging the transition to cleaner energy
They have no impact on energy markets
They only exist in developing countries
They make renewable energy more competitive

quiz_questions.explanation: Fossil fuel subsidies, estimated at hundreds of billions of dollars globally, keep fossil fuel prices artificially low, making it harder for renewable energy to compete on price and slowing the transition to clean energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What was the primary energy policy achievement of Denmark by the 2020s?

options.text: Generating over 50% of its electricity from wind power
Becoming the world's largest oil producer
Building the most nuclear power plants in Europe
Eliminating all forms of taxation on energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Denmark became a global leader in wind energy, with wind turbines generating more than 50% of the country's electricity consumption, demonstrating that high levels of wind penetration are technically feasible.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an externality in the context of energy economics?

options.text: A tax paid by energy companies to the government
The cost of importing energy from foreign countries
A cost or benefit that affects a party not directly involved in the economic transaction
The price of energy on the international market

quiz_questions.explanation: An externality is a cost or benefit imposed on third parties who did not choose to incur it. Air pollution from a coal plant that harms nearby residents' health is a negative externality not reflected in the price of electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'energy security' mean in a national policy context?

options.text: Having guards protect power plants from terrorism
A nation's ability to ensure a reliable and affordable supply of energy
The encryption used to protect smart grid data
The insurance policy on energy infrastructure

quiz_questions.explanation: Energy security refers to a nation's capacity to maintain a reliable, adequate, and affordable energy supply, often involving diversification of energy sources and reducing dependence on imported fuels from politically unstable regions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by the term 'energy justice'?

options.text: Legal proceedings against energy companies
The right of energy companies to build wherever they choose
The equitable distribution of energy benefits and burdens across all communities, including marginalized populations
A legal standard requiring all homes to have electricity

quiz_questions.explanation: Energy justice addresses the fair distribution of both the benefits of energy systems (affordable access) and their burdens (pollution, health impacts), which disproportionately affect low-income communities and communities of color.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'stranded asset' in the context of fossil fuel policy?

options.text: An investment in fossil fuel infrastructure that loses value due to the energy transition or climate policy
A renewable energy project that fails to generate electricity
An oil tanker that runs aground and cannot be recovered
A power plant located in a remote area without road access

quiz_questions.explanation: Stranded assets are fossil fuel reserves or infrastructure (such as coal plants or oil wells) that become economically unviable before the end of their expected lifetime due to climate regulations, carbon pricing, or competition from cheaper renewables.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'just transition' mean in energy policy?

options.text: A legal requirement for equal numbers of fossil fuel and renewable energy plants
A rapid and immediate shutdown of all fossil fuel operations
A transition that only considers the needs of energy companies
A policy framework that ensures workers and communities dependent on fossil fuels are supported during the shift to clean energy

quiz_questions.explanation: A just transition ensures that coal miners, oil workers, and fossil-fuel-dependent communities receive retraining, economic assistance, and new employment opportunities so they are not left behind as the economy shifts to clean energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'carbon neutrality'?

options.text: Eliminating all carbon from industrial processes
Using carbon-neutral paint on buildings
Having a neutral opinion about carbon taxes
Achieving a balance between emitting carbon and absorbing carbon from the atmosphere

quiz_questions.explanation: Carbon neutrality means that the net carbon dioxide emissions produced by a person, organization, or country are zero, achieved by balancing emissions with carbon removal through offsets, forests, or carbon capture technology.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a Renewable Energy Certificate (REC)?

options.text: A warranty certificate for wind turbine equipment
A tradable certificate representing proof that one megawatt-hour of electricity was generated from a renewable source
An academic degree in renewable energy engineering
A license required to install solar panels on a home

quiz_questions.explanation: A REC is a market-based instrument certifying that one megawatt-hour of electricity was generated from renewable sources. RECs can be bought and sold, allowing companies to claim renewable energy use even if their local grid relies on fossil fuels.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'energy poverty' mean?

options.text: An energy company that is going bankrupt
A shortage of fossil fuels in a region
The lack of access to reliable, affordable, and clean energy services
The decline in energy stock prices

quiz_questions.explanation: Energy poverty affects an estimated 760 million people globally who lack access to electricity and billions more who rely on polluting fuels for cooking, leading to health problems, limited economic opportunity, and environmental degradation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'decarbonization' in energy policy?

options.text: Extracting carbon from the atmosphere to make diamonds
Banning all carbon-containing materials from manufacturing
The process of reducing carbon dioxide emissions from the energy system and economy
Removing carbon filters from water treatment plants

quiz_questions.explanation: Decarbonization refers to the systematic reduction of carbon dioxide emissions from energy production, transportation, industry, and other sectors, typically through switching from fossil fuels to low-carbon energy sources.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'base load power' mean?

options.text: The electricity used to power a military base
The minimum level of electricity demand that must be met continuously over a 24-hour period
Emergency backup power during blackouts
The maximum electricity demand during peak hours

quiz_questions.explanation: Base load is the constant, minimum amount of electric power delivered or required 24 hours a day. Base load plants, such as nuclear and large coal plants, run continuously to meet this consistent demand.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coal-dependent region in Appalachia faces massive job losses as coal mines close due to declining demand and competition from natural gas and renewables. The state government has $500 million to invest in economic transition. Which policy approach would most effectively support the affected communities?

options.text: Subsidize coal mining to keep mines open regardless of market conditions
Provide one-time severance payments with no other support
Relocate all affected workers to other states
Invest in retraining programs, renewable energy manufacturing, broadband infrastructure, and economic diversification

quiz_questions.explanation: A comprehensive just transition strategy invests in workforce retraining, attracts new industries like renewable energy manufacturing, and builds infrastructure that enables economic diversification, creating sustainable long-term opportunities for affected communities.

quiz_questions.question_text: The European Union implemented its Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) in 2005. During the first phase, carbon prices dropped to near zero because too many free permits were issued. What lesson does this teach about designing cap-and-trade systems?

options.text: Free permits should be given to all companies permanently
Carbon prices should always be set by the government rather than the market
The cap must be set stringently enough to create real scarcity of permits, or prices will be too low to drive change
Cap-and-trade systems can never work effectively

quiz_questions.explanation: The EU ETS experience demonstrated that an oversupply of emission permits leads to low carbon prices that provide no incentive to reduce emissions. Later phases tightened the cap and reduced free allocations, raising prices and improving effectiveness.

quiz_questions.question_text: China is simultaneously the world's largest carbon emitter and the world's largest investor in renewable energy. A policy analyst argues that China's energy strategy demonstrates a key tension in developing-nation energy policy. What is that tension?

options.text: The disagreement between urban and rural populations about energy sources
The competition between solar and wind energy companies
The conflict between military spending and energy investment
The tension between meeting immediate economic growth and energy needs while transitioning to sustainable sources

quiz_questions.explanation: China exemplifies how rapidly developing nations face the challenge of meeting enormous current energy demand (often with fossil fuels) while simultaneously investing in the clean energy infrastructure needed for a sustainable future.

quiz_questions.question_text: After the 2011 Fukushima disaster, Germany announced it would close all nuclear power plants by 2022. Critics argue this forced greater reliance on coal in the short term. What does this case study illustrate about energy policy trade-offs?

options.text: Phasing out one low-carbon source without sufficient renewable capacity can temporarily increase fossil fuel dependence and emissions
Germany should have built more coal plants instead
Nuclear energy should never be considered as part of the energy mix
Energy policy decisions should not be influenced by safety concerns

quiz_questions.explanation: Germany's nuclear phase-out illustrates that removing one low-carbon energy source before adequate renewable replacement capacity is built can paradoxically increase emissions if the gap is filled by fossil fuels.

quiz_questions.question_text: A small island developing state faces rising sea levels from climate change and depends entirely on imported diesel for electricity. The government wants to transition to 100% renewable energy but lacks the financial resources. Which international policy mechanism could help?

options.text: Climate finance mechanisms like the Green Climate Fund that provide grants and loans to vulnerable nations for clean energy projects
Building a nuclear power plant with domestic funding
Reducing electricity consumption to zero
Increasing diesel imports from multiple countries for energy security

quiz_questions.explanation: International climate finance mechanisms, such as the Green Climate Fund established under the UNFCCC, channel resources from developed to developing nations to support mitigation and adaptation projects, including renewable energy transitions.

quiz_questions.question_text: British Columbia, Canada, implemented a revenue-neutral carbon tax in 2008, returning all revenue to residents through tax cuts. Studies found that the province's fuel consumption dropped by 5-15% while its economy grew at the same pace as the rest of Canada. What does this case study demonstrate?

options.text: Carbon taxes always destroy economic growth
A well-designed carbon tax can reduce emissions without harming economic growth
Revenue-neutral carbon taxes have no effect on emissions
British Columbia's economy grew only because of the carbon tax

quiz_questions.explanation: British Columbia's experience provides evidence that a carbon tax can effectively reduce fossil fuel consumption while maintaining economic competitiveness, particularly when revenue is returned to taxpayers through offsetting tax reductions.

quiz_questions.question_text: India launched the International Solar Alliance in 2015 to promote solar energy in tropical countries. The initiative aims to mobilize $1 trillion in solar investments by 2030. What strategic advantage do tropical countries have for solar energy development?

options.text: They have lower electricity demand
They have more fossil fuel reserves to supplement solar
They receive high levels of solar irradiance year-round due to their geographic location near the equator
They have longer power transmission lines

quiz_questions.explanation: Tropical countries between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn receive the most consistent and intense solar radiation on Earth, giving them a natural advantage for solar energy production with higher capacity factors than locations at higher latitudes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas became the leading U.S. state in wind energy production despite being traditionally associated with the oil and gas industry. What market-based factor most contributed to this development?

options.text: Texas banned all oil and gas production
Abundant wind resources, available land, and a deregulated electricity market that attracted private investment
The federal government forced Texas to build wind farms
Oil companies converted their drilling rigs into wind turbines

quiz_questions.explanation: Texas's combination of excellent wind resources in west Texas and the Gulf Coast, vast open land, and a competitive deregulated electricity market created ideal conditions for wind energy investment without requiring heavy government mandates.

quiz_questions.question_text: A developing nation discovers a massive offshore natural gas reserve. Environmental groups argue the country should leave it in the ground and invest in renewables instead. The government argues the gas revenue is needed to fund development. How can energy policy balance these competing priorities?

options.text: Extract all the gas as quickly as possible and ignore environmental concerns
Develop the gas reserve with strong environmental standards while investing a significant portion of revenues in renewable energy infrastructure
Leave the gas in the ground and rely on foreign aid for development
Sell the drilling rights to a foreign company and use none of the revenue for energy transition

quiz_questions.explanation: A balanced approach uses natural gas revenue to fund the transition to renewables while minimizing environmental damage through strict extraction standards. This pragmatic strategy acknowledges development needs while building long-term clean energy capacity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Norway, a major oil and gas exporter, has achieved nearly 100% renewable electricity domestically through hydropower and has the world's highest electric vehicle adoption rate. Critics call this contradictory since Norway profits from selling fossil fuels to other countries. What energy policy dilemma does this illustrate?

options.text: Hydropower is not actually a renewable energy source
Electric vehicles are worse for the environment than gasoline cars
Norway's approach proves that all countries should export fossil fuels
A nation can achieve domestic decarbonization while still contributing to global emissions through fossil fuel exports

quiz_questions.explanation: Norway's case highlights the tension between domestic climate action and global responsibility. While Norway has decarbonized its own electricity and transportation, its oil and gas exports contribute significantly to emissions in other countries, raising questions about supply-side climate policy.
